coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence method for quickly detecting ochratoxin A in flour and beer using nitrogen doped carbon dots and silver nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chengke
Tan, Rong
Chen, Dan
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a FRET (Forster resonance energy transfer) based fluorescence method was developed for the quickly detection of ochratoxin A (OTA) in agricultural products (e.g., flour and beer). A highly fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (CD) were served as energy donor, the DNA and MCH (6-mercapto-1-hexanol) modified Ag nanoparticles were served as energy acceptor in the FRET system. OTA can be detected in a concentration range between 10 and 5000 nM, the limit of detection is 8.7 nM. This method has three advantages: (1) an enhanced fluorescent intensity can be acquired by utilizing the nitrogen doped CD synthesized by one-step approach without sophisticated modification of nanoparticles; (2) OTA detection was accomplished quickly (less than 30 min) by using MCH as assistant molecule; (3) an extended OTA detection linear range was acquired, which may facilitate the OTA detection in real agricultural samples, and is helpful for solving food safety problems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver nanoparticle
Carbon dots
Ochratoxin A
Fluorescence method
Flour

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of OTA in aqueous solutions
Detection of OTA in flour and beer
The characterization of cDNA-CD and Apt-AgNPs
The mechanism of the OTA detection
The detection of OTA in aqueous solutions
The selectivity and robustness of FRET based method
OTA detection in flour and beer

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21305032 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2014M551522 ), Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral Research Funds (No. 1402073B ) and Senior Talent Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 13JDG069 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of AgNPs and CD. The excitation (emission wavelength at 436 nm) and emission (excitation wavelength at 360 nm) spectra of CD (A), inset of (A) indicated the images of CD under daylight (left) and ultra-violet light (right, 365 nm), respectively. The overlapped absorption spectrum of Apt-AgNPs (black, solid line) and fluorescence emission spectrum of cDNA-CD (red, dotted line), respectively (B), the TEM images of AgNPs (C) and CD (D), respectively, inset of (D) indicated the HRTEM images and lattice of CD.
Schematic illustration of the FRET based fluorescence method for detecting OTA in beer and flour. The illustration is not drawn to scale.

The optimization of experimental conditions. The emission spectra of cDNA-CD in the absence (red, short line), presence of unmodified AgNPs (blue, dotted line) and Apt-AgNPs (black, solid line), respectively (A); the differential of fluorescent emission intensity of Apt-AgNPs and cDNA-CD solutions at 436 nm in the absence and presence of 1000 nM OTA as a function of the ratio of Apt-AgNPs and cDNA-CD (B), and the pH value of solutions (C), respectively. The changes of fluorescence emission intensity of the cDNA-CD and Apt-AgNPs solutions at 436 nm as a function of the reaction time after the addition of 1000 nM OTA (D). The error bars represent the relative standard deviation of three experimental results.

The fluorescence spectra of Apt-AgNPs and cDNA-CD solutions in the presence of different concentrations of OTA (A), the corresponding fluorescence intensity of solutions in (A) at 436 nm as a function of the concentrations of OTA (B), inset of (B) shows the corresponding images of the solutions in (A), from left to right refers to the concentration of OTA increasing from 0 to 5000 nM, respectively. The error bars represent the relative standard deviation of three experimental results.

The selectivity of this experiment. The fluorescent spectra of Apt-AgNPs and cDNA-CD solutions after the addition of 1000 nM different kinds of interferences (A) and the corresponding fluorescent emission intensities at 436 nm (from left to right) of each solutions (B), respectively. The error bars represent the relative standard deviation of three experimental results.
The comparison of this fluorescent method with previous reports.
Determination of OTA concentrations in flour and beer samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorescence method for quickly detecting ochratoxin A in flour and beer using nitrogen doped carbon dots and silver nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a FRET (Forster resonance energy transfer) based fluorescence method was developed for the quickly detection of ochratoxin A (OTA) in agricultural products (e.g., flour and beer). A highly fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (CD) were served as energy donor, the DNA and MCH (6-mercapto-1-hexanol) modified Ag nanoparticles were served as energy acceptor in the FRET system. OTA can be detected in a concentration range between 10 and 5000 nM, the limit of detection is 8.7 nM. This method has three advantages: (1) an enhanced fluorescent intensity can be acquired by utilizing the nitrogen doped CD synthesized by one-step approach without sophisticated modification of nanoparticles; (2) OTA detection was accomplished quickly (less than 30 min) by using MCH as assistant molecule; (3) an extended OTA detection linear range was acquired, which may facilitate the OTA detection in real agricultural samples, and is helpful for solving food safety problems.

ce:section.ce:para: Ochratoxin A (OTA) is a kind of mycotoxin which is often found in moldy or fermentative agricultural products [1], it is severely harmful to human body, such as renal toxicity, liver toxicity, et al., especially to the people lived in humid and warm environments (e.g., south of Asia and north of Africa) [2,3]. Therefore, it is valuable to sensitively and quickly detect OTA in agricultural and food species. Nowadays, the traditional methods to detect OTA including the chromatography measurement (such as liquid chromatography (LC) or liquid chromatography-mass spectrograph (LC-MS) methods) [4,5] and antibody based enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [6,7], which play an important role in the detection and control of OTA in many food related species. However, the relative expensive instrument, sophisticated and time-consuming experimental procedures restrict their widely use in OTA detection for food safety control.

Recently, aptamer was getting more and more attention for the small molecules detection, since it is easy to acquire, has relatively low price and stability, has strong affinity with target. Therefore, the aptasensor based colorimetric method [8–13], fluorescent method [14–19] and electrochemical method [20–23] had been widely employed to detect OTA. For example, OTA detection based on the color change of gold nanoparticle solution [10] or chromogenic reaction catalyzed by DNAzyme [9,24] after the addition of OTA; OTA detection based on the specific interaction of OTA with the aptamer on the surface of electrode, which induced the impedance of electrode changed a lot [21,25]; OTA detection based on the fluorescence signal recovery of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from aggregated state to dispersed state [26]. These methods have many advantages, such as easy to conduct, without complicated operation, avoid using expensive antibody and many sophisticated instruments. However, some improvements are still needed for these methods; the main purposes are as follows: save the detection time, extend the detection linear range, increase the accuracy of the detection, and simplify the experimental procedures, enhance the detection signal, et al. New functional materials and smart experimental design are needed to fulfill these requirements. Recently, carbon dot (CD) had got great attention as it can be easily prepared and functionalized with different functional groups (i.e., -COOH and-NH2), has stable fluorescent property, therefore, it has been widely used in fluorescent assay for detecting various compounds [12,13,27–30]. Especially, the distance-dependent fluorescent quenching property makes carbon dots suitable for constructing FRET (Forster resonance energy transfer) systems, to effectively enhance the sensitivity of the detection [31]. Recently, Guo et. al. had used DNA functionalized CD to sensitively detect OTA, the fluorescent intensity of CDs deceased dramatically when they formed aggregates, while after the addition of OTA, the aggregates were broken, accompanied with the fluorescent signal of CD solution enhanced, consequently [26]. Although this method shows high sensitivity, the detection time (more than 2 h) should be shortened, the detection linear range (0–1 ng/mL) should be extended, to facilitate the quickly detection of real samples with different concentrations of OTA.

In this experiment, we would like to develop a method to quickly detect OTA in agricultural products with enhanced sensitivity and wide detection range. Meanwhile, by using aptamer as a versatile platform, FRET as a common approach, we hope this method can also be used for other small molecules detection. In this method, a FRET system contained silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and CD was proposed based on the hybridization reaction between two DNA strands (aptamer and complementary DNA (cDNA)) on the two probes, respectively. The fluorescent intensity of carbon dots was decreased as a result of the approaching of CD and AgNPs, while the addition of OTA made the fluorescent signal of CD solutions recovered, consequently. In this system, a higher fluorescent intensity can be obtained using nitrogen doped carbon dots; a shorter interaction time was achieved by using MCH (6-mercapto-1-hexanol) as the assistant molecule, as well as the design of tail-to-tail DNA hybridization patterns to decrease the steric hindrance; a wider detection linear range was obtained, which may originate from the strong interaction of aptamer and cDNA. These advantages would facilitate the detection of real agricultural samples contained diverse concentrations of OTA.

AgNO3, MCH, ammonium citrate, 1-Ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); Tris-hydroxymethyl aminomethane (Tris), ascorbic acid, trisodium citrate, citric acid, kanamycin sulfate, sisomicin sulfate, tobramycin sulfate, spectinomycin sulfate and acetamiprid were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Zearalenone and aflatoxin M1 were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (USA). OTA was purchased from Pribolab Inc. (Singapore), OTA aptamer [26] (5′-GATCG GGTGT GGGTG GCGTA AAGGG AGCAT CGGAC ACGCC ACCCA CACA-C6H12-SH-3′) and cDNA (5′-CCT TTA CGC CAC CCA CAC CCG ATC-C6H12-NH2-3′) were obtained from Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The flour and beer samples were purchased from a local supermarket. ELISA kit for OTA was obtained from CusaBio Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Wuhan, China) and was used according to the manufacturer's instruction. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Double distilled water (dd H2O) was used throughout the experiment.

The UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a Tecan Infinite 200 PRO microplate reader (Mannedorf, Swiss). pH values of the solutions were adjusted using a Leici PHS-3E pH meter (INESA Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., China). The centrifugation process was operated using a CF-10 centrifuge (WiseSpin Co., Ltd., Korea). All measurements were carried out at room temperature otherwise mentioned specially.

AgNPs were synthesized according to previous report with minor modification [32] and stored at 4 °C. Briefly, 5 mg ascorbic acid and 40 mg trisodium citrate were mixed in 48 mL H2O, the pH of the solution was adjusted using NaOH to 11, then, 2 mL AgNO3 (10 mg) was added dropwise on ice-water bath. After stirred for 30 min, the solution was transferred into a 100 °C water bath for 1 h. Then, 3 mL as prepared AgNPs (the concentration of AgNPs is calculated as 120 μg/mL based on previous report [32]) were centrifugated, and the supernatant was discarded, then 1 mL AgNPs was added into the precipitate to acquire a concentrated solution, 50 μL thiolated aptamer (10 μM) and 125 μL Tris-HCl (10 mM, pH 7.4) were added into this solution, and reacted for 24 h at room temperature; then equal volume of NaCl (50 mM) was slowly added into this solution, and put onto a TU-100C digital dry bath incubator (Bluepard, Co. Ltd, China) to slowly evaporate water until the final volume reached at 1/4 of the original solution. Then, Tris-HCl solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) was added to make the final volume is 1 mL. 50 μL MCH (10 mM) was added into this solution afterwards (the high concentration of MCH are sufficient for Ag decoration based on the amounts of MCH, aptamer and AgNPs [32]) and reacted for 30 min at room temperature. The final mixture was centrifuged (13,500 r/min) for 30 min to remove the excess aptamer and MCH, the precipitate was washed with 1 mL Tris-HCl for 2 times, and dispersed into 1 mL Tris-HCl and stored at 4 °C for the future use.

The carbon dots were synthesized using citrate acid and ammonium citrate as the precursor according to previous reports with minor modification, respectively [33,34]. Briefly, 0.5 g ammonium citrate or citrate acid was added into 15 mL H2O, and heated to 200 °C for 30 min, then, the pH value of the solution was adjusted to 8.0 using NaOH. Then 50 μL (4 M) EDC and 50 μL (4 M) NHS were added into 5 mL as prepared carbon dots without further purification (ammonium citrate as the precursor, here the solutions are not purified as the carbon dots are homogeneous according to the microscopic results) and mixed sufficiently, then sonicated for 15 min, 125 μL (10 μM) cDNA was added into this solution and reacted for 40 min at room temperature, and centrifugated at 13,500 r/min for 1 h to remove the unreacted cDNA, and dispersed into the PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4, 50 mM NaCl) for the future use.
XPS spectra were collected using an Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (Thermo, UK). Carbon dots solution was dropped on a Si substrate and oven-dried before measurement.

The TEM micrographs of carbon dots were obtained by a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOS, Japan) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. And the TEM micrographs of AgNPs were obtained by a Tecnai 12 transmission electron microscope (FEI, Netherlands) operated at an accelerating voltage of 120 kV. Specimens were prepared by putting a droplet of sample solution on a carbon-coated copper mesh grid and dried in the air.
The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (4000–650 cm−1) were recorded with a Nicolet IS10 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Co., Ltd., USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTA in aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para: 50 μL PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4, 50 mM NaCl), 30 μL Ca2+ (10 mM) and 100 μL cDNA-CD were added into 200 μL Apt-Ag for 10 min, then different concentrations of OTA were added and reacted for 13 min at room temperature, then, the fluorescent spectra were recorded, the excitation and emission wavelengths are 360 nm and 436 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTA in flour and beer
ce:section.ce:para: The pretreatment process of flour was following previous reports [24]. Briefly, 4 mL diluted methanol (the ratio of methanol to H2O is 4:1) was added into 1 g flour which contained different amounts of OTA, and sonicated for 5 min, then the mixtures were centrifuged at 13,000 r/min for 5 min, 1 mL supernatant was added into 2 mL Tris-HCl (10 mM, pH 7.4), and used for OTA detection. For the OTA detection in beer, different amounts of OTA were added into beer, and 10 μL of the mixture was added into 990 μL PBS, then the solutions were detected using above mentioned method. In order to verify the nanoparticle based method, the concentrations of OTA in the above-mentioned samples were also determined using commercial OTA ELISA kit following the manufacture's instruction. Briefly, the OTA standard solution (provided by the manufacture, working concentration range is 0–1000 nM) and the pretreated real sample solution (diluted by 1 and 20 times using buffer solution, respectively) were added into the wells of ELISA plate, after incubation for 1 h, the supernatant was discarded, and the enzyme linked second antibody was added and react for 1 h after washing the plate wells, after discarding the supernatant, the chromogenic reagent (provided by the manufacture) was added and react for 30 min, then the termination reagent (provided by the manufacture) was added, the absorption of the final solutions were recorded. Based on the calibration curve (according to the instruction of manufacture), the concentration of the OTA in the detection solution were calculated and compared with the nanoparticle based method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of cDNA-CD and Apt-AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1A, the CD synthesized by ammonium citrate has a typical fluorescent spectrum, which has a maximum excitation wavelength at 360 nm and a maximum emission wavelength at 436 nm. The photo in the inset of Fig. 1A indicated that the synthesized CD has a brown color under daylight and deep-blue color under ultra-violet light at 365 nm, correspondingly. The CD also has different maximum emission wavelength and red-shifted when the excitation wavelengths increased, consequently, which is the characteristics of carbon dots, and is consistent with the previous reports that the emission wavelength of CD is correlated to the excitation wavelength, and is not same as the traditional fluorescent molecular probes (i.e., fluorescein), which has definite maximum excitation and emission wavelength (as shown in Fig. S1A) [29,33]. In order to demonstrate the merit of the nitrogen-doped carbon dots, carbon dots without nitrogen doping were also synthesized using citrate acid (without nitrogen element) as precursor, as shown in Fig. S1B, the fluorescent intensity of carbon dots without nitrogen is much lower than the nitrogen doped carbon dots, this result is consistent with the previous reports that doping with heteroatoms may enhance the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots, and is originated from the defects mechanism and isolated sp2 conjugated carbon structures [35]. In general, these experimental results indicated that the fluorescent carbon dots were prepared and the fluorescence intensity can be enhanced by doping with nitrogen element.

In order to form a donor-acceptor couple in a FRET system, AgNPs was also synthesized, as shown in Fig. S2A, the AgNPs has a maximum absorption wavelength at 397 nm. The emission intensity of CD solutions has a negligible change after it was functionalized by cDNA (see Fig. S2B), which means the absorption property of AgNPs was not disturbed after decorated with DNA. Meanwhile, after functionalized by DNA, a new absorbance peak at 260 nm can be found for cDNA-CD and Apt-AgNPs (as shown in Figs. S2A and C), which is corresponding to the absorbance peak of cDNA or aptamer, and indicated that the aptamer and cDNA were successfully attached onto the surface of these probes.

TEM measurements were utilized to further study the morphology of CD and AgNPs, as shown in Fig. 1C and D, the diameters of AgNPs and CD are about 15 nm and 4 nm, respectively, the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CD in the inset of Fig. 1D shows the CD with lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, which is the characteristics of CD, and is consistent with the previous report [26], these experimental results demonstrate that the CD and AgNPs were successfully prepared. We also utilized XPS measurement to determine the element constitute of CD, as shown in Figs. S3A-C, there are C1s, N1s and O1s peaks, which corresponding to the C-C, C˭O, C-N and -COOH groups, and indicated that nitrogen element was successfully doped into CDs. FTIR spectrum (see Fig. S3D) also shows there are C˭O (1716.14 cm−1), -OH (1377.58 cm−1, 2840.70 cm−1) and N-H (1548.55 cm−1) groups [36]. Above all, these experimental results confirmed that the nitrogen doped CD was successfully prepared and the -NH2 functionalized cDNA could conjugate onto the CD surface through -NH2 and -COOH interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of the OTA detection
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1B, the absorbance spectra of Apt-AgNPs is well overlapped with the emission spectrum of cDNA-CD, this phenomenon facilitated the FRET between AgNPs and CD, and made the fluorescent signals of CD deceased as a result of energy transferring, consequently. After adding OTA into this system, as the OTA can specifically interact with the aptamer conjugated on AgNPs, which makes the de-hybridization of cDNA and aptamer, and the separation of Apt-AgNPs with cDNA-CD. With the distance between AgNPs and CD increased, the FRET between these two particles was inhibited, and the fluorescent intensity was enhanced with increasing the concentration of OTA (see Fig. 2). By this means, the OTA can be quantitatively detected based on the fluorescent signal intensity changes.

As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescent intensity of cDNA-CD was not changed a lot after unmodified AgNPs were added, while after the addition of Apt-AgNPs, the fluorescent intensity of the mixture was significantly decreased with increasing the interaction time from 0 to 10 min, and kept stable after 10 min (see Fig. S4), these experimental results confirmed the proposed FRET mechanism. Meanwhile, the experimental results also indicated that the interaction between cDNA-CD and Apt-AgNPs is faster compared with previous reports, which may cost more than 2 h in the hybridization process [26]. The ratio of Apt-AgNPs and cDNA-CD is also important for the OTA detection, as shown in Fig. 3B and S5A-B, the differentials of fluorescent emission intensity of solutions in the absence and presence of 1 μM OTA are increased with increasing the ratio of Apt-AgNPs and cDNA-CD from 0.5:1 to 2:1, and decreased from 2:1 to 5:1, this may because that at low ratio of these two probes, the FRET effect are not significant enough to decrease the cDNA-CD fluorescent intensity; while at higher ratio of these two probes (above 2:1), more OTA were required to competitively interact with Apt-AgNPs, which will also decrease the sensitivity of the detection. The pH of the solutions was also evaluated in order to see its effect on the detection of OTA, as shown in Fig. 3C and S5C-D, with increasing the pH of the solution from 5 to 9.4, the optimal pH was at pH 7.4, this may because the stability of N-doped CD was decreased at lower pH, which interfere with the combination of cDNA-CD and Apt-AgNPs; while at higher pH value, the coordination of OTA by aptamer was also inhibited [9]. Meanwhile, the Ca2+ may also precipitate at higher pH value, which decreased the competitive interaction ability of OTA with aptamer compared with cDNA [24]. The interaction time after adding OTA into Apt-AgNPs solutions was also studied, as shown in Fig. 3D and S6, the fluorescent intensity was increased with increasing the reaction time and kept stable after 13 min. Above all, the ratio of Apt-AgNPs to cDNA-CD at 2:1, the pH value of solutions at 7.4 and the reaction time between OTA and Apt-AgNPs at 13 min were selected in the following experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of OTA in aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Next, OTA was detected in aqueous solutions under selected optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 4, the fluorescent intensities at 436 nm were increased with increasing the concentration of OTA from 10 to 5000 nM with a calibration curve as I = 0.0466 [OTA]/nM + 240.243 with the correlation coefficient as R2 = 0.98, the limit of detection is calculated as 8.7 nM (3 times of standard deviation of the blank (i.e., 0.13514)/the slope of the calibration curve (i.e., 0.0466), 3σ), while the limit of quantification (LOQ) is also calculated as 29 nM (10σ) [37,38]. Here the I referred as the fluorescence intensity of the final solutions with the excitation wavelength at 360 nm and the emission wavelength at 436 nm, and the [OTA] referred as the concentration of OTA in the solution. The image in the Fig. 4B (inset) also shows the fluorescent intensities of solutions increased with increasing the concentrations of OTA from 0 to 5000 nM (the solution without OTA was also provided here for comparison).

Compared with previous reports (see Table 1), this FRET based method shows reasonable sensitivity. Especially, this method is fast (the interaction between cDNA-CD and Apt-AgNPs can finish within 10 min, and the competitive interaction between OTA and Apt-AgNPs can finish within 13 min), this may because that aptamer and MCH are co-conjugated onto AgNPs. Here, the MCH other than other assistant molecules (e.g., polyethylene glycol) was selected as it was frequently used in many electrochemical experiments [20,21,30], it has a relatively low price and was easily acquired, which may facilitate the construction of the surface of functional probes. In these studies, MCH was used for enhancing the performance of functional electrode, as the addition of MCH can significantly decrease the non-specific adsorption of biomolecules and can maintain the natural structure and bioactivity of conjugated functional molecules, as well as decrease the steric hindrance of the reaction between different molecules. Therefore, in this study, we speculated that the MCH may not only increase the stability of AgNPs by decreasing the Van der Waals' force between AgNPs, but also decrease the steric hindrance when the Apt-AgNPs interact with cDNA-CD, which is consistent with previous reports that shorter assistant molecules made the functional DNA more flexible on the surface of nanoparticles, and easier to hybridize with complementary DNA [39]. Meanwhile, the sensitivity of this method is also profit from the designed distance-sensitive FRET protocol and tail-to-tail arrangement of DNA strand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity and robustness of FRET based method
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the selectivity of this method, different kinds of interferences were added into the solution, as shown in Fig. 5, the fluorescent intensity of the solutions enhanced only if OTA was exist, while other compounds (including some antibiotics and other mycotoxins with similar structures) show negligible interferences at the same concentration of OTA. The experimental result indicated that the synthesized probes are specific to OTA, this is because the selected aptamer on the surface of AgNPs can specifically interact with OTA. Meanwhile, in order to show the stability and reliability of this assay, the OTA detection experiment was conducted for 7 times in a week (with three replicates, once per day). As shown in Fig. S7, according to the experimental result, the fluorescence signal of the solution and the detected amounts of OTA in the solution are relatively constant, and is similar to the added amounts of OTA (i.e., 1000 nM) in the original solution, this result indicated that this method has reasonable accuracy and robustness. Therefore, we demonstrate that this method has wide linear detection range, good specificity, low LOD and LOQ, as well as the satisfactory precision and repeatability, it may be used for the real sample detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: OTA detection in flour and beer
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate that this method can be used in the detection of OTA in real samples, in addition to the OTA is often found in moldy cereal or fermenting samples, it is valuable to detect OTA in these agricultural species [24]. After pretreated by methanol or just by dilution the beer using buffered solution, OTA in flour and beer were detected using above mentioned method and traditional ELISA method (based on the manufacture's instruction) respectively by standard additional procedures. As shown in Table 2, four concentrations of OTA were added into the real samples, the recovery ratio of OTA in these species detected by fluorescence method is between 98.7% and 113.1% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) under 6.27%, and the concentrations of OTA detected by these two methods are consistent with each other. These experimental results indicated that the FRET based method has the potential to be used for the detection of OTA in real samples.

In summary, based on the distance-sensitive FRET mechanism and fluorescent property of nitrogen doped CD, a fluorescence method was developed for OTA detection. This method has reasonable sensitivity, selectivity and stability, as well as satisfactory recoveries in flour and beer samples, as low as 10 nM OTA can be detected, the wide linear detection range is between 10 and 5000 nM, and the detection signal maintain stable in 7 continuous days. Compared with previous reported OTA detection methods, this method has at least three advantages, 1) an enhanced fluorescent intensity which benefit from the nitrogen doped carbon dots, 2) a less experimental time which benefit from the utility of assistant molecule (i.e., MCH) to decrease the steric hindrance and to keep the natural structure of DNA, 3) an extended detection linear range (three magnitudes). Although this proof-in-concept method was utilized for OTA detection, we anticipated that this assay can be further improved by using other functional hybrid materials with superior properties (e.g., magnetic nanoparticles to decrease the background signal, catalytic materials or nanomaterials with stronger fluorescence to further enhance the detection signal), and may be transferrable to deal with other analytical problems (as aptamer is a good platform to recognize small molecules), such as the quantitatively determine other mycotoxins, pesticides, even pathogenic bacteria in food, which is meaningful to the food safety analysis and management.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21305032), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2014M551522), Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral Research Funds (No. 1402073B) and Senior Talent Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 13JDG069).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.02.007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ochratoxin A: general overview and actual molecular status

Methods of Analysis for Ochratoxin A, Mycotoxins and Food Safety
Development of a sensitive enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay for the determination of ochratoxin A
A simple and rapid biosensor for ochratoxin A based on a structure-switching signaling aptamer
Aptamer-DNAzyme hairpins for biosensing of ochratoxin A
Aptamer-based colorimetric biosensing of Ochratoxin A using unmodified gold nanoparticles indicator
Determination of ochratoxin A with a DNA aptamer
A dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric "light on" sensor for thiocyanate based on fluorescent carbon dots and unmodified gold nanoparticles
Carbon dots-assisted colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode protocol for acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibitors screening based on the inner filter effect of silver nanoparticles
Highly fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots for an inner filter effect-based cyanide sensor
Single-walled carbon nanotubes based quenching of free FAM-aptamer for selective determination of ochratoxin A
A simple and sensitive approach for ochratoxin A detection using a label-free fluorescent aptasensor
PVP-coated graphene oxide for selective determination of ochratoxin A via quenching fluorescence of free aptamer
A fluorescent aptasensor based on a DNA pyramid nanostructure for ultrasensitive detection of ochratoxin A
Fluorescence analyzer based on smartphone camera and wireless for detection of ochratoxin A
Electrochemical DNA aptamer-based biosensor for OTA detection, using superparamagnetic nanoparticles
An electrochemical competitive biosensor for ochratoxin A based on a DNA biotinylated aptamer
Electrochemical sensor for discrimination tyrosine enantiomers using graphene quantum dots and beta-cyclodextrins composites
Resonance energy transfer based electrochemiluminescence and fluorescence sensing of riboflavin using graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots
Label-free colorimetric aptasensor for sensitive detection of ochratoxin A utilizing hybridization chain reaction
Simply amplified electrochemical aptasensor of ochratoxin A based on exonuclease-catalyzed target recycling
Tuning the aggregation/disaggregation behavior of graphene quantum dots by structure-switching aptamer for high-sensitivity fluorescent ochratoxin A sensor

Carbon dots based fluorescent sensor for sensitive determination of hydroquinone

Development of a carbon dot (C-Dot)-linked immunosorbent assay for the detection of human α-fetoprotein

Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg (Ⅱ) ions
Detection and quantitation of mycotoxins in infant cereals in the US market by LC-MS/MS using a stable isotope dilution assay
